cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Priya, a buy-side analyst, spends the week assembling public filings and industry shipment data, then calls a former plant employee who tells her the exact unreleased quarterly earnings figure, which is far below guidance. Combining everything, she concludes earnings will disappoint and sells the company out of her clients' portfolios.
vignette_b: Priya, a buy-side analyst, spends the week assembling public filings and industry shipment data, then calls a former plant employee who tells her the loading dock has been unusually quiet lately, an individually non-material observation. Combining everything, she concludes earnings will disappoint and sells the company out of her clients' portfolios.
decisive_fact: Whether the nonpublic detail the former employee provided was the exact unreleased earnings figure (material nonpublic information) or merely an individually non-material observation about dock activity.
distractors: Whether Priya traded for her clients' accounts rather than her own.
Whether Priya spent a full week gathering public filings and shipment data before acting.
Whether the source Priya contacted was a former employee rather than a current one.
rationale: Standard II(A) prohibits acting on material nonpublic information, but the mosaic theory permits conclusions drawn from public information combined with individually non-material nonpublic details. In vignette_a the former employee handed Priya the exact unreleased earnings figure — material nonpublic information — so trading on it violates II(A); in vignette_b the same call yields only a non-material observation about quiet dock activity, so her conclusion is a permissible mosaic. The distractors (trading for clients, doing a week of public research, or the source being a former employee) are identical or near-identical across both cases and do not change the outcome.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: A discretionary manager places a large, illiquid, highly speculative private placement into the account of a retiree whose written investment policy statement specifies capital preservation and income, with withdrawals expected within a year. The manager is convinced the deal will outperform.
vignette_b: A discretionary manager places the same illiquid, speculative private placement into the account of a client whose written investment policy statement expressly authorizes high-risk illiquid alternatives, who has a long horizon and ample liquidity elsewhere, after confirming the position fits the stated mandate.
decisive_fact: Whether the investment was consistent with the individual client's written investment policy statement — objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints.
distractors: Whether the private placement was speculative and illiquid.
Whether the manager had discretionary authority over the account.
Whether the manager genuinely believed the deal would outperform.
rationale: Standard III(C) requires investments to suit the client's objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints as captured in the IPS, judged in a total-portfolio context. The same security can be unsuitable for one client and suitable for another — suitability is client-relative, not a fixed property of the security or a matter of the manager's conviction. The decisive flip is consistency with the client's IPS.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Rahim, an analyst covering Vantex Corp, pieces together a highly bullish view of the stock by combining Vantex's public filings with a confidential, still-secret projection that a Vantex executive privately handed him showing a pending, market-moving earnings surge. Before that projection becomes public, he phones his college roommate and tells him to buy Vantex shares.
vignette_b: Rahim, an analyst covering Vantex Corp, pieces together a highly bullish view of the stock by combining Vantex's public filings with several individually non-material observations he gathered from public suppliers and customers about a pending, market-moving earnings surge. Before that view becomes widely known, he phones his college roommate and tells him to buy Vantex shares.
decisive_fact: Whether Rahim's bullish conclusion rested on a confidential, still-secret material projection privately handed to him by a Vantex executive, or was assembled solely from public filings plus individually non-material public observations under the mosaic theory.
distractors: Whether the person Rahim phoned to buy the shares was his college roommate.
Whether the anticipated earnings surge was market-moving and would raise the share price.
Whether Rahim conveyed the tip before the information became public rather than after.
rationale: Standard II(A) bars acting or causing others to act on material nonpublic information, but the mosaic theory permits conclusions built from public information combined with individually non-material nonpublic pieces. In A the view rests on a confidential, still-secret material projection given by an insider, so tipping his roommate causes another to act on MNPI and violates II(A); in B the identical bullish conclusion is assembled solely from public filings plus non-material public observations, so acting on it is permitted mosaic-theory analysis and conforms. The flip turns entirely on the material-nonpublic versus mosaic character of the underlying information; the roommate's identity, the size of the expected price move, and the pre-announcement timing are common to both cases and do not change the outcome.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Lena manages an equity fund at an integrated bank whose information barrier keeps deal information on the banking side. Over lunch, an investment-banking colleague breaches the barrier and tells her a bank client will launch a hostile bid for Orvel Industries next month, and that afternoon she buys Orvel for her fund.
vignette_b: Lena manages an equity fund at an integrated bank whose information barrier keeps deal information on the banking side. Over lunch, an investment-banking colleague discusses only the weather and never mentions any deal, and that afternoon she buys Orvel for her fund based on her own published valuation work.
decisive_fact: Whether Lena possessed the material nonpublic hostile-bid information when she traded, because her banking colleague conveyed it to her rather than saying nothing about the deal.
distractors: Whether Lena worked at an integrated bank that also had an investment-banking division.
Whether Lena had lunch with an investment-banking colleague before placing the trade.
Whether the trade was placed on the same afternoon as the lunch conversation.
rationale: Standard II(A) prohibits trading while in possession of material nonpublic information. In vignette A the colleague conveys the pending hostile-bid information, so Lena possesses MNPI when she buys Orvel and violates II(A); in vignette B the colleague says nothing about any deal, so Lena never obtains MNPI and her purchase rests solely on her own public analysis, which conforms. The single flip is whether the MNPI actually reached Lena; the existence of a banking division, the fact that a lunch occurred, and the same-day timing of the order are constant across both scenarios and do not change the outcome.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Tomas, a sell-side analyst, reads a single report from an unaffiliated boutique claiming Halcyon Metals has doubled its ore reserves, and without checking any of it, immediately issues a strong buy on Halcyon citing those reserves.
vignette_b: Tomas, a sell-side analyst, reads the boutique's report claiming Halcyon Metals doubled its ore reserves, then independently corroborates the figures against the company's audited filings and an independent geologist's survey before issuing his strong buy.
decisive_fact: Whether Tomas relied solely on the single unverified third-party report, or independently corroborated its key reserve claims before recommending the stock.
distractors: Whether the third-party report came from an unaffiliated boutique firm.
Whether the recommendation issued was a strong buy in both cases.
Whether the claim concerned a doubling of ore reserves specifically.
rationale: Standard V(A) requires a reasonable and adequate basis, supported by research and investigation, for any recommendation. Issuing a buy on the strength of one unverified outside report lacks a reasonable basis and violates V(A), while independently corroborating the key claims against audited filings and an independent survey establishes that basis and conforms. The decisive flip is the verification of the source's central claims, not who authored the report, the rating level, or the subject of the claim.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Devin, a portfolio manager, recommends a complex structured note to clients based on a third-party pricing model whose embedded volatility and correlation assumptions he cannot explain and has never examined.
vignette_b: Devin, a portfolio manager, recommends the same complex structured note only after studying the pricing model's volatility and correlation assumptions, stress-testing its outputs, and satisfying himself that the assumptions are sound.
decisive_fact: Whether Devin understood and tested the assumptions underlying the pricing model, or recommended the product without grasping how the model worked.
distractors: Whether the recommended instrument was a complex structured note.
Whether the model used was developed by a third party rather than in-house.
Whether the model incorporated volatility and correlation inputs.
rationale: Standard V(A) requires diligence and a reasonable basis, which cannot exist when a member relies on a model whose assumptions he does not understand. Recommending the note without grasping the model violates V(A), while testing and understanding the assumptions first supplies the required basis and conforms. The decisive flip is the manager's understanding and testing of the model's assumptions, not the product's complexity, the model's third-party origin, or the presence of volatility inputs.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Nadia, an advisor, learns that a client recently lost his job and now needs near-term liquidity, but she nonetheless applies the firm's standard growth-tilted model portfolio to his account without revisiting his written IPS.
vignette_b: Nadia, an advisor, learns that a client recently lost his job and now needs near-term liquidity, so she first updates his written IPS to reflect the changed circumstances and only then applies a model portfolio consistent with the revised objectives and constraints.
decisive_fact: Whether Nadia updated the client's written IPS to reflect his materially changed circumstances before applying a model portfolio, or applied the standard model without revisiting the IPS.
distractors: Whether the portfolio applied was the firm's standard model rather than a custom one.
Whether the client had recently lost his job.
Whether the model portfolio was growth-tilted in orientation.
rationale: Standard III(C) requires that suitability be judged against the client's current written IPS, which must be updated as circumstances change. Applying a standard model without reflecting the client's new liquidity needs violates III(C), while updating the IPS first and then matching the portfolio to the revised constraints conforms. The decisive flip is whether the IPS was updated to the changed circumstances, not the use of a model portfolio, the fact of the job loss alone, or the portfolio's growth tilt.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: A brokerage client with a conservative IPS phones Sergio and demands he buy a highly leveraged inverse ETF that plainly conflicts with her stated risk tolerance; Sergio executes the unsolicited order immediately without any discussion.
vignette_b: A brokerage client with a conservative IPS phones Sergio and demands he buy the same highly leveraged inverse ETF that plainly conflicts with her risk tolerance; Sergio first discusses the suitability mismatch with her and documents that conversation before executing the unsolicited order.
decisive_fact: Whether Sergio discussed and documented the suitability mismatch with the client before executing the unsolicited unsuitable trade, or executed it immediately without any discussion.
distractors: Whether the trade was initiated by the client rather than recommended by Sergio.
Whether the instrument was a highly leveraged inverse ETF.
Whether the client held a conservative investment policy statement.
rationale: Standard III(C) directs that, for unsolicited trades that are unsuitable, the member discuss and document the suitability concern before executing. Executing the unsuitable order with no discussion violates III(C), while discussing the mismatch and documenting it first conforms even though the trade is still placed. The decisive flip is the pre-execution discussion and documentation, not that the client initiated the order, the leveraged nature of the product, or the client's conservative IPS.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Fiona's college roommate, now Crestline Foods' CFO, privately tells her over dinner that the board has just signed a definitive agreement to be acquired next week at a large premium, information not yet announced. On the strength of that tip she buys Crestline shares for her clients before the announcement.
vignette_b: Fiona assembles Crestline Foods' public filings, publicly reported plant expansions, and several individually non-material observations from distributors, and from this mosaic concludes an acquisition is likely and buys Crestline shares for her clients before any announcement.
decisive_fact: Whether Fiona traded on a confidential tip of a signed, unannounced acquisition received directly from Crestline's CFO, or on a mosaic of public information and individually non-material observations.
distractors: Whether Fiona was trading for client accounts rather than her own account.
Whether the anticipated acquisition carried a large premium.
Whether the trades were placed before any public announcement was made.
rationale: Standard II(A) prohibits acting on material nonpublic information; a confidential tip from the target's CFO about a signed but unannounced acquisition is both material (it would move the price) and nonpublic (obtained from an insider with a duty of confidentiality), so trading on it violates II(A). Reaching the same conclusion by synthesizing public filings with individually non-material details is the mosaic theory, which II(A) expressly permits. The decisive flip is the source and character of the information—an insider's tip of confirmed MNPI versus a lawful public mosaic—not whether client accounts were used, the size of the premium, or that both trades preceded the announcement.

cluster_label: III(C) Suitability · II(A) MNPI · V(A) Diligence
vignette_a: Marcus manages assets for a pension whose written mandate authorizes a currency-hedging derivative overlay to manage foreign-exchange exposure; he rejects a proposed overlay solely because derivatives strike him as risky in isolation, ignoring that it reduces total-portfolio risk under the mandate.
vignette_b: Marcus manages assets for a pension whose written mandate authorizes a currency-hedging derivative overlay; he evaluates a proposed overlay against the mandate and constraints in a total-portfolio context, confirms it lowers overall risk, and implements it accordingly.
decisive_fact: Whether Marcus judged the overlay's suitability against the client's stated mandate in a total-portfolio context, or rejected it solely because derivatives looked risky viewed in isolation.
distractors: Whether the instrument in question was a derivative overlay.
Whether the client was an institutional pension rather than an individual.
Whether the overlay was intended to hedge foreign-exchange exposure.
rationale: Standard III(C) requires suitability to be assessed against the client's written mandate and constraints in a total-portfolio context, not by judging an instrument's riskiness in isolation. Rejecting a mandate-authorized, risk-reducing overlay merely because derivatives seem risky misapplies the standard and violates III(C), while evaluating it against the mandate and total-portfolio risk conforms. The decisive flip is the total-portfolio, mandate-relative assessment, not that the instrument is a derivative, that the client is institutional, or that the overlay hedges currency.

vignette_a: Analyst Dana Ortiz prepares a client note and, without any citation, incorporates a section she copied word-for-word from another firm's research report. The copied section is that firm's own original macroeconomic forecast and interpretation of where rates are headed.
vignette_b: Analyst Dana Ortiz prepares a client note and, without any citation, incorporates a section she copied word-for-word from a published report. The copied section is a table of GDP and inflation figures released by a recognized government statistical agency.
decisive_fact: Whether the uncited copied section is another firm's original analysis and forecast (which requires attribution) or factual data compiled by a recognized government statistical agency (which may be used without citing each figure).
distractors: Whether Dana copied the section word-for-word rather than rewording it in her own style.
Whether the note was distributed to clients rather than kept for internal use only.
Whether the copied section appeared at the front of the note rather than in an appendix.
rationale: Under Standard I(C), presenting another party's original analysis as one's own without attribution is plagiarism, so copying the firm's proprietary forecast without citation violates the Standard; by contrast, factual data from a recognized statistical or reporting service may be used without citing each figure, so copying the agency's published GDP and inflation numbers conforms. The decisive flip is the nature of the copied material — original analysis versus recognized factual data — not that it was copied verbatim, distributed to clients, or placed prominently, since those facts hold in both vignettes and do not change the outcome.

vignette_a: Pitching a five-year structured note to prospective clients, adviser Dara Okafor states that the product 'guarantees you will get an 8% return each year.' The note's return is linked to an equity index and is not fixed or contractually assured by the issuer.
vignette_b: Pitching a five-year structured note to prospective clients, adviser Dara Okafor states that the product 'guarantees you will get an 8% return each year.' The note's return is fixed and contractually assured at 8% annually by the issuer under its written terms.
decisive_fact: Whether the promised 8% return is a fixed amount contractually assured by the issuer, or is linked to an equity index and therefore fluctuates.
distractors: Whether the stated return figure was 8% rather than some lower percentage.
Whether the statement was made to prospective clients rather than to existing clients.
Whether the product being pitched was a five-year note rather than a shorter-term one.
rationale: Under Standard I(C), guaranteeing a return on an investment whose value inherently fluctuates is a misrepresentation, whereas accurately stating a guarantee that genuinely exists by contract is a factual statement, not a violation. The flip is driven solely by whether the 8% is contractually assured by the issuer (conform) or is index-linked and thus fluctuating (violate). The size of the figure, the audience (prospective vs. existing clients), and the note's tenor do not change the outcome.

vignette_a: Reuben commissions an independent writer to produce a detailed sector report, strips the writer's byline, and distributes it to his clients under his own name as his personal work.
vignette_b: Reuben commissions an independent writer to produce a detailed sector report and distributes it to his clients with a note clearly identifying the outside author as the report's originator.
decisive_fact: Whether the outside author's work was presented as Reuben's own by removing the credit, or was distributed with clear attribution to the outside author.
distractors: Whether Reuben paid the writer for the report rather than obtaining it for free.
Whether the report covered a sector rather than an individual security.
Whether the report was sent to clients rather than kept for internal use.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits presenting another party's original work as one's own; removing the author credit and issuing the report under Reuben's name is plagiarism and a misrepresentation, whereas clearly attributing it to the outside author is not. The decisive flip is the presence or absence of attribution, not that the writer was paid, that a sector was covered, or that clients received it.

vignette_a: Having recently joined Crestline Advisors, Priya markets to prospects the strong five-year returns she helped produce at her former firm, presenting the figures without indicating they were earned at any prior employer.
vignette_b: Having recently joined Crestline Advisors, Priya markets to prospects the strong five-year returns she helped produce at her former firm, presenting the figures with a clear label that they were earned while she was employed at her prior firm.
decisive_fact: Whether the prior-firm returns were presented with no indication of where they were earned, or were clearly labeled as performance earned at her prior firm.
distractors: Whether the marketed track record spanned five years rather than a shorter period.
Whether Priya had recently changed employers.
Whether the returns were shown to prospects rather than existing clients.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits misrepresenting one's performance history; presenting prior-firm results without indicating they were earned elsewhere misleads prospects into thinking they reflect work done at the current firm, while clearly labeling them as prior-firm performance is a permissible, accurate representation. The decisive flip is solely whether the results are attributed to the prior firm, not the length of the record, the recent job change, or whether the audience is prospects rather than existing clients.

vignette_a: Meridian Capital publishes a composite return for its value strategy that quietly excludes several terminated and money-losing accounts, boosting the reported figure above what all qualifying accounts actually earned.
vignette_b: Meridian Capital publishes a composite return for its value strategy that includes every account meeting the strategy's definition, including terminated and money-losing accounts, reflecting the full set of qualifying results.
decisive_fact: Whether terminated and losing accounts were quietly omitted to inflate the composite, or all qualifying accounts were included so the composite is complete.
distractors: Whether the composite was for a value strategy rather than a growth strategy.
Whether the figure was published rather than shared privately.
Whether some of the excluded accounts had been terminated by clients.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits misrepresenting performance; selectively omitting terminated and losing accounts overstates the composite and misleads readers, while presenting the complete set of qualifying accounts is a fair representation. The decisive flip is the completeness of the composite, not the strategy type, the fact of publication, or that some accounts were terminated.

vignette_a: On his marketing bio, Damir states that he personally managed the flagship Meridian Growth Fund for eight years; in reality he spent those eight years as a junior analyst who never held managerial responsibility for the fund.
vignette_b: On his marketing bio, Damir states that he personally managed the flagship Meridian Growth Fund for eight years; in reality he did spend those eight years as the fund's manager with full managerial responsibility for it.
decisive_fact: Whether Damir actually held the managerial role over the fund that his bio claims, or was in reality only a junior analyst who never managed it.
distractors: Whether the bio was used for external marketing rather than kept as an internal record.
Whether the fund he referenced was described as the firm's flagship fund.
Whether Damir stated his tenure with the fund as eight years.
rationale: Under Standard I(C), misrepresenting one's professional role and qualifications is a misrepresentation, so claiming to have personally managed a fund one never managed violates the Standard, whereas stating a role that is genuinely true does not. The flip turns solely on whether the claimed managerial role matches reality; the marketing context, the fund's flagship status, and the eight-year tenure are identical across both vignettes and therefore do not drive the outcome.

vignette_a: In a client presentation, Oksana reproduces a rival firm's distinctive proprietary valuation chart, an original analytical framework the rival developed, without any credit, implying it is her own work.
vignette_b: In a client presentation, Oksana reproduces a chart of raw commodity price series pulled from a recognized market data vendor without footnoting each individual data point.
decisive_fact: Whether the reproduced chart was a rival's original proprietary analytical framework used without credit, or a chart of raw factual data from a recognized data vendor.
distractors: Whether the chart appeared in a client presentation rather than a written report.
Whether Oksana reformatted the chart's colors and layout.
Whether the rival firm was a direct competitor.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits presenting another's original analysis as one's own; copying a rival's proprietary analytical framework without credit is plagiarism, whereas presenting raw factual data from a recognized vendor does not require footnoting each figure. The decisive flip is whether the chart embodies original proprietary analysis versus recognized factual data, not the presentation format, cosmetic reformatting, or the rival's competitive status.

vignette_a: Writing a market outlook, Tobias inserts several sentences taken word-for-word from an external strategist's published note into his own commentary with no quotation marks and no attribution, so they read as his own analysis.
vignette_b: Writing a market outlook, Tobias includes several sentences from an external strategist's published note set off in quotation marks and clearly attributed to that strategist by name.
decisive_fact: Whether the strategist's sentences were reproduced without quotation or attribution, or were quoted and clearly attributed to the strategist.
distractors: Whether the borrowed material consisted of several sentences rather than a single line.
Whether the source note had been published rather than confidential.
Whether Tobias's outlook was distributed widely.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits presenting another's words as one's own; copying the strategist's sentences without quotation marks or attribution is plagiarism, while quoting them with clear attribution is proper use. The decisive flip is whether the material is quoted and attributed, not the number of sentences, the fact that the source was published, or the breadth of distribution.

vignette_a: Pitching a structured note to prospective investors, Naledi states that the note's principal is fully guaranteed, but the note carries no such principal guarantee and its value can fall with the underlying index.
vignette_b: Pitching a structured note to prospective investors, Naledi states that the note's principal is fully guaranteed, and the note carries a written principal guarantee from the issuer covering the full amount.
decisive_fact: Whether a principal guarantee genuinely exists by contract (the issuer's written guarantee covers the full amount) or does not exist (the note carries no such guarantee and can lose value).
distractors: Whether the pitch was made to prospective investors rather than to current clients.
Whether the product was a structured note rather than a conventional bond or fund.
Whether Naledi made the guarantee claim verbally in a sales pitch rather than in a written prospectus.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits statements that misrepresent an investment's characteristics. Telling investors the principal is 'fully guaranteed' when no such guarantee exists — the note can lose value with the index — is a misrepresentation and violates I(C); making the identical statement when a written issuer guarantee actually covers the full principal is factually accurate and conforms. The decisive flip is whether the guarantee genuinely exists by contract, not whether the audience was prospects, the product was a structured note, or the claim was made verbally.

vignette_a: Farrah's firm sends clients a newsletter recommending a small-cap stock, framing it as the product of the firm's in-house research team, when the recommendation was actually supplied wholesale by an unaffiliated third-party research provider.
vignette_b: Farrah's firm sends clients a newsletter recommending a small-cap stock, clearly disclosing that the recommendation originated with an unaffiliated third-party research provider whose report the firm is passing along.
decisive_fact: Whether the third party's recommendation was passed off as the firm's own in-house research, or was disclosed as originating with the unaffiliated third-party provider.
distractors: Whether the recommended security was a small-cap stock.
Whether the recommendation was delivered through a client newsletter.
Whether the third-party provider charged the firm for the research.
rationale: Standard I(C) prohibits misrepresenting the source of one's work; implying that outsourced third-party research is the firm's own in-house analysis is a misrepresentation, while disclosing the true third-party source is permissible. The decisive flip is whether the true source is disclosed, not the type of security, the newsletter format, or whether the firm paid for the research.

vignette_a: A portfolio manager buys a block of a thinly traded stock for his own account in the morning, then places the client buy orders for the same stock that afternoon; the client orders push the price up, improving the fill on his earlier personal purchase.
vignette_b: A portfolio manager first completes the client buy orders for a thinly traded stock, and only after all client orders are filled — following his firm's disclosed procedures — does he buy the same stock for his own account.
decisive_fact: Whether the manager traded for his own account before or after the client orders were executed.
distractors: Whether the stock was thinly traded.
Whether the manager had disclosed his personal holdings to clients.
Whether the manager ultimately made a profit on the position.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over transactions in which the member has a beneficial interest. Buying personally before filling client orders — and profiting from the price impact of those client orders — disadvantages clients and violates VI(B). Filling clients first and only then trading personally respects client priority. The decisive flip is the timing of the personal trade; disclosure and profitability do not cure trading ahead.

vignette_a: Allotted a limited number of shares in an oversubscribed, sought-after IPO, a manager directs a large slice to his spouse's account, which he controls and in which he has a beneficial interest, leaving eligible clients with reduced allocations.
vignette_b: Allotted a limited number of shares in an oversubscribed IPO, a manager distributes them pro rata to all eligible client accounts under a pre-established written allocation policy and excludes his own and his family's beneficial accounts from the scarce allocation.
decisive_fact: Whether the scarce IPO shares were allocated to the manager's beneficial (family) account ahead of, or instead of, eligible clients.
distractors: Whether the IPO was oversubscribed.
Whether the spouse independently qualified as an eligible investor.
Whether the manager personally received a large dollar gain.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires client transactions to take precedence over transactions for accounts in which the member has a beneficial interest, including controlled family accounts. Diverting scarce IPO shares to a beneficial account at clients' expense subordinates clients and violates VI(B); a fair pre-established pro-rata allocation that puts clients first conforms. The decisive flip is whether beneficial accounts were favored over clients in the scarce allocation.

vignette_a: Analyst Priya Menon plans to place a large client block buy in Halcyon Semiconductors this week. On Monday morning she buys call options on Halcyon in her personal brokerage account, then submits the client block order Monday afternoon.
vignette_b: Analyst Priya Menon plans to place a large client block buy in Halcyon Semiconductors this week. She first submits and completes the client block order on Monday, and only after all client shares are filled does she buy call options on Halcyon in her personal account.
decisive_fact: Whether Menon bought the personal call options before or after the client block order was executed.
distractors: Whether the personal position was in options rather than the underlying shares.
Whether the client order was a large block.
Whether Halcyon was a semiconductor company.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over transactions in which the member has a beneficial interest. Establishing a personal options position ahead of a client block she is about to place lets Menon profit from the client-driven price move and subordinates clients, violating VI(B); trading personally only after clients are fully filled respects client priority. The decisive flip is the timing relative to the client order — the use of options, the block size, and the issuer's industry do not change the outcome.

vignette_a: Portfolio manager Diego Ferreira manages several client accounts and a brokerage account belonging to his brother, which Diego controls and in which he shares beneficial interest. When a limited buy fills partially, he gives his brother's account the best-priced early fills before the client accounts.
vignette_b: Portfolio manager Diego Ferreira manages several client accounts and a brokerage account belonging to his brother, which Diego controls and in which he shares beneficial interest. When a limited buy fills partially, he allocates fills to all client accounts first and treats his brother's account exactly like the clients, with no preferential timing or pricing.
decisive_fact: Whether the controlled family account received preferential fills ahead of clients or was treated the same as clients.
distractors: Whether the account belonged to a family member rather than the manager himself.
Whether the order filled only partially.
Whether Diego managed several client accounts.
rationale: Standard VI(B) treats an account the member controls and holds a beneficial interest in — such as a family account — like the member's own, so it must not be favored over clients. Giving the brother's beneficial account the best fills ahead of clients subordinates clients and violates VI(B); treating it identically to clients conforms. The decisive flip is preferential versus equal treatment of the beneficial account — that it is a relative's account, that the fill was partial, and the number of clients do not by themselves determine the outcome.

vignette_a: An oversubscribed secondary offering of Brightwater Utilities allots a scarce number of shares to manager Sofia Larsen's firm. Sofia takes a personal allocation for her own account before determining what her eligible clients will receive.
vignette_b: An oversubscribed secondary offering of Brightwater Utilities allots a scarce number of shares to manager Sofia Larsen's firm. Sofia allocates the full scarce amount to eligible clients first and takes a personal allocation only after all eligible clients have been satisfied.
decisive_fact: Whether Sofia took her personal allocation before or after eligible clients were allocated the scarce shares.
distractors: Whether the offering was a secondary offering rather than an IPO.
Whether the offering was oversubscribed.
Whether Brightwater operated in the utilities sector.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires that client transactions take precedence over transactions in which the member has a beneficial interest. Claiming a personal allocation of scarce offering shares before clients are satisfied diverts a limited opportunity away from clients and violates VI(B); taking a personal allocation only after clients are fully allocated conforms. The decisive flip is the sequence of the personal allocation relative to clients — that it was a secondary rather than an IPO, that it was oversubscribed, and the issuer's sector do not change the outcome.

vignette_a: Manager Owen Nakamura sends clients a written notice disclosing that he personally holds Cedar Grid Power. He then buys additional shares for his own account just before entering the day's client buy orders in the same stock.
vignette_b: Manager Owen Nakamura sends clients a written notice disclosing that he personally holds Cedar Grid Power. He then enters and completes the day's client buy orders in the stock first, and only afterward buys additional shares for his own account.
decisive_fact: Whether Owen bought for his own account before or after the client orders were executed.
distractors: Whether Owen disclosed his personal holding to clients in writing.
Whether the disclosure notice was in writing rather than verbal.
Whether Owen already held a position in the stock.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over the member's beneficial transactions, and disclosure of a conflict under VI(A) does not by itself license trading ahead of clients. Buying personally before the client orders — even with prior disclosure — subordinates clients and violates VI(B); filling clients first and trading personally afterward conforms. The decisive flip is the timing of the personal trade — the existence, form, and prior holding referenced in the disclosure do not cure front-running.

vignette_a: Portfolio manager Lena Kowalski obtains her firm's required written pre-clearance to trade Ironpeak Logistics both personally and for clients. On the morning she begins building the client position, she fills her own personal Ironpeak order first and then places the client buy orders.
vignette_b: Portfolio manager Lena Kowalski obtains her firm's required written pre-clearance to trade Ironpeak Logistics both personally and for clients. On the morning she begins building the client position, she fills the client buy orders first and then places her own personal Ironpeak order.
decisive_fact: Whether Lena filled her own personal Ironpeak order before the client orders or after them.
distractors: Whether Lena had obtained the firm's required written pre-clearance for the trades.
Whether Ironpeak Logistics was a logistics company.
Whether Lena executed both the personal and client trades on the same morning.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires that the member's own (beneficial-interest) transactions yield to client transactions. Obtaining the firm's required pre-clearance merely authorizes Lena to trade the security; it does not entitle her to fill her personal order ahead of clients, so doing so violates VI(B), while filling clients first and trading personally afterward conforms. The decisive flip is the timing of the personal fill relative to clients — the pre-clearance, the issuer's industry, and same-day execution do not cure trading ahead.

vignette_a: A limited block buy in Northwind Chemicals fills only partially. Manager Rafael Ortega, who has combined his own order with the client orders, allocates the available shares to his personal account first and leaves the clients with whatever remains.
vignette_b: A limited block buy in Northwind Chemicals fills only partially. Manager Rafael Ortega, who has combined his own order with the client orders, allocates the available shares pro rata across the client accounts and takes any personal allocation last.
decisive_fact: Whether the partial fill went to Rafael's own account first or was allocated pro rata to clients with his account last.
distractors: Whether Rafael combined his personal order with the client orders.
Whether the block filled only partially.
Whether Northwind operated in the chemicals sector.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over transactions in which the member has a beneficial interest. Allocating a partial fill to his own account first leaves clients disadvantaged and violates VI(B); allocating pro rata to clients and taking any personal shares last respects client priority. The decisive flip is who receives the scarce partial fill first — the fact that he bunched orders, that the fill was partial, and the issuer's sector do not by themselves determine the outcome.

vignette_a: Portfolio manager Tomas Berg is filling a large buy order in Solace Devices for Client A. While that order is still working, he places and fills a Solace buy order for Client B, an unrelated advisory client in whose account Tomas holds no beneficial interest and from which he does not personally benefit.
vignette_b: Portfolio manager Tomas Berg is filling a large buy order in Solace Devices for Client A. While that order is still working, he places and fills a Solace buy order for a personal account that Tomas controls and in which he holds a beneficial interest, letting it fill ahead of the client order.
decisive_fact: Whether the account that traded ahead of Client A was one Tomas controlled and held a beneficial interest in (his personal account), or an unrelated client account in which he had no beneficial interest.
distractors: Whether the second order was filled while Client A's large order was still working.
Whether the security involved was Solace Devices, a consumer-device maker.
Whether the account that traded ahead was filled in full rather than in part.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires that transactions in an account in which the member has a beneficial interest yield to client transactions; it does not bar a manager from trading for one client while another client's order is still working. Filling an unrelated client's order (in which Tomas has no beneficial interest) ahead of Client A is permissible under VI(B), whereas letting his own beneficial account fill ahead of the client subordinates the client and violates VI(B). The decisive flip is whether the account trading ahead was a beneficial-interest (personal) account — not the concurrent timing, the security involved, or whether the fill was complete.

vignette_a: Manager Ingrid Solberg receives a favorable buy signal for Marlowe Foods for a client. She deliberately delays entering the client's order until her own personal buy in Marlowe has filled at the better price, then submits the client order.
vignette_b: Manager Ingrid Solberg receives a favorable buy signal for Marlowe Foods for a client. She promptly executes the client's order at the favorable price and only afterward submits her own personal buy in Marlowe.
decisive_fact: Whether Ingrid delayed the client's order so her personal trade filled first or executed the client promptly before trading personally.
distractors: Whether Ingrid personally traded in the same stock as the client.
Whether the buy signal was favorable.
Whether Marlowe was a food company.
rationale: Standard VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over the member's beneficial transactions. Delaying the client's favorable order so her own trade fills first at the better price disadvantages the client and violates VI(B); executing the client promptly and trading personally afterward conforms. The decisive flip is whether the client order was delayed in favor of the personal trade — that she traded in the same name, that the signal was favorable, and the issuer's industry do not change the outcome.
